perturbation.original_text: Each Party may disclose the other Party’s Confidential Information to its directors, officers, members, managers, employees, legal advisors, and financial advisors (each bound by obligations of confidentiality consistent with this Agreement) who have bona fide need to know, but only to the extent necessary to carry out the Business Purpose. Each Party agrees to instruct all such representatives not to disclose such Confidential Information to third parties, including consultants, without the prior written permission of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party may disclose the other Party’s Confidential Information to its directors, officers, members, managers, employees, legal advisors, and financial advisors without any restrictions, even without a bona fide need to know or relevance to the Business Purpose. The Receiving party is not required to obtain written permission from the Disclosing Party before sharing Confidential Information with third parties, including consultants. Representatives are free to share information without limitation.
perturbation.explanation: By modifying Section 4 to allow disclosure of Confidential Information to representatives 'without any restrictions' and removing the requirement for 'bona fide need to know' and prior written permission for third-party disclosure, this text creates an in-text contradiction with Section 3(c), which mandates that each Party 'not to disclose such Confidential Information to third parties.' This change directly undermines the core confidentiality obligations outlined in Section 3, leading to uncertainty about the permissible scope of disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Party agrees: (a) to hold the other Party’s Confidential Information in strict confidence; (b) to exercise at least the same care in protecting the other Party’s Confidential Information from disclosure as the Party uses with regard to its own Confidential Information; (c) not to disclose such Confidential Information to third parties; and Page 2 of 7 (d) not to use any Confidential Information for any purpose except for the Business Purpose without the prior, written consent of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 4 explicitly allows for the disclosure of confidential information without any restrictions, a 'bona fide need to know,' or prior written permission for third-party disclosure. This directly contradicts Section 3(c), which states that each party agrees 'not to disclose such Confidential Information to third parties.' The original text required written permission to share with 3rd parties, and had a bona fide need to know. The changed text removed both of those requirements, making the changed text a direct contradiction to the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees not to remove any materials or tangible items containing any of the other party’s Confidential Information from the premises of the Disclosing Party without the Disclosing Party’s consent. Each Party agrees to comply with any and all terms and conditions that the Disclosing Party imposes upon approved removal of such materials or items, including, without limitation, that the removed materials or items must be returned by a certain date, and that no copies of the removed materials or items are to be made.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party has the unrestricted right to remove any materials or tangible items containing the other party's Confidential Information from the premises of the Disclosing Party, with no obligation to obtain the Disclosing Party's consent. The Receiving Party is not required to comply with any terms or conditions imposed by the Disclosing Party upon removal of such materials or items, including, without limitation, returning materials by a certain date or restricting the making of copies.
perturbation.explanation: By modifying Section 6 to allow unrestricted removal of Confidential Information without the Disclosing Party's consent or compliance with their terms, the text creates an in-text contradiction with Section 3(a) & (b), which requires each Party 'to hold the other Party’s Confidential Information in strict confidence' and 'to exercise at least the same care in protecting the other Party’s Confidential Information from disclosure as the Party uses with regard to its own Confidential Information'. Allowing unrestricted removal directly conflicts with the obligation to maintain strict confidence and exercise due care.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Party agrees: (a) to hold the other Party’s Confidential Information in strict confidence; (b) to exercise at least the same care in protecting the other Party’s Confidential Information from disclosure as the Party uses with regard to its own Confidential Information; (c) not to disclose such Confidential Information to third parties; and Page 2 of 7 (d) not to use any Confidential Information for any purpose except for the Business Purpose without the prior, written consent of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 6 grants a party the unrestricted right to remove confidential information without consent or adhering to the Disclosing Party's terms. This directly contradicts Section 3(a) & (b), which mandates strict confidentiality and exercising the same care in protecting confidential information as one's own. The original text required consent for removal, but the changed text explicitly removes this requirement, causing a clear and strong in-text contradiction, making it impossible to enforce both sections.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the Disclosing Party’s request, the Receiving Party will promptly return to the Disclosing Party all materials or tangible items containing the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information and all copies thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party is under no obligation to return any materials or tangible items containing the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information, even upon the Disclosing Party's request. The Receiving Party may retain all such materials and copies indefinitely, without any requirement for return.
perturbation.explanation: By modifying Section 7 to state that the Receiving Party is 'under no obligation to return' Confidential Information, even upon request, the text creates an in-text contradiction with Section 11, which acknowledges that 'all of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential information is owned solely by the Disclosing Party (and/or its licensors)'. This change directly undermines the Disclosing Party's ownership rights and ability to control its Confidential Information, as recognized in Section 11, because the Receiving Party can simply keep it.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Party acknowledges that all of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential information is owned solely by the Disclosing Party (and/or its licensors) and that the unauthorized disclosure or use of such Confidential Information would cause irreparable harm and significant injury, the degree of which may be difficult to ascertain and for which monetary damages would provide an insufficient remedy. Accordingly, each Party agrees that the Disclosing Party shall have the right to obtain an immediate injunction from any court of competent jurisdiction enjoining actual or threatened breach of this Agreement and/or actual or threatened disclosure of the Confidential Information without the necessity of posting a bond or other security. Each Party shall also have the right to pursue any other rights or remedies available at law or equity for such a breach.
perturbation.justification: The changed text states that the Receiving Party has no obligation to return confidential information, even upon request. The contradicted text states that the Disclosing Party solely owns the confidential information. This creates a direct contradiction because the Receiving Party's ability to retain the information indefinitely directly undermines the Disclosing Party's ownership rights as stated in the contradicted text. The Perturbation Explanation further clarifies this point.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall notify the Disclosing Party immediately upon discovery of any unauthorized use or disclosure of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information, or any breach of the Agreement by the Receiving Party, and will cooperate with the Disclosing Party in any reasonable fashion in order to assist the Disclosing Party regain possession of the Confidential Information and prevent its further unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party is not obligated to notify the Disclosing Party of any unauthorized use or disclosure of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information, nor is the Receiving Party required to cooperate with the Disclosing Party in any fashion to regain possession of the Confidential Information or prevent its further unauthorized use or disclosure. The Receiving party has no duty to report or mitigate breaches.
perturbation.explanation: By modifying Section 8 to remove the Receiving Party's obligation to notify the Disclosing Party of unauthorized use or disclosure, this text creates an in-text contradiction with Section 11, which states that 'the unauthorized disclosure or use of such Confidential Information would cause irreparable harm and significant injury'. Removing the notification requirement hinders the Disclosing Party's ability to mitigate potential harm from unauthorized use, which Section 11 implicitly recognizes.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Party acknowledges that all of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential information is owned solely by the Disclosing Party (and/or its licensors) and that the unauthorized disclosure or use of such Confidential Information would cause irreparable harm and significant injury, the degree of which may be difficult to ascertain and for which monetary damages would provide an insufficient remedy. Accordingly, each Party agrees that the Disclosing Party shall have the right to obtain an immediate injunction from any court of competent jurisdiction enjoining actual or threatened breach of this Agreement and/or actual or threatened disclosure of the Confidential Information without the necessity of posting a bond or other security. Each Party shall also have the right to pursue any other rights or remedies available at law or equity for such a breach.
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that the Receiving Party is not obligated to notify the Disclosing Party of any unauthorized use or disclosure of confidential information. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which acknowledges that 'the unauthorized disclosure or use of such Confidential Information would cause irreparable harm and significant injury'. By removing the notification obligation, the Receiving Party is no longer required to take action to prevent the 'irreparable harm and significant injury' that the contradicted section recognizes, leading to a strong inconsistency and unenforceability of the original terms, as the Disclosing Party is hindered in mitigating potential harm.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party recognizes and agrees that nothing contained in this Agreement will be construed as granting any rights to the Receiving Party, by license or otherwise, to use any of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information except as specified in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges and agrees that the Receiving Party is automatically granted an unrestricted, perpetual, and royalty-free license to use any of the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information for any purpose whatsoever, irrespective of the terms outlined elsewhere in this Agreement. The receiving party owns any derivative work.
perturbation.explanation: By modifying Section 9 to grant the Receiving Party an 'unrestricted, perpetual, and royalty-free license' to use the Confidential Information 'for any purpose whatsoever,' the text creates an in-text contradiction with Section 3(d), which states that the Receiving Party may 'not to use any Confidential Information for any purpose except for the Business Purpose without the prior, written consent of the Disclosing Party.' This complete reversal of usage rights creates significant ambiguity and directly conflicts with the purpose stated in the document.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Party agrees: (a) to hold the other Party’s Confidential Information in strict confidence; (b) to exercise at least the same care in protecting the other Party’s Confidential Information from disclosure as the Party uses with regard to its own Confidential Information; (c) not to disclose such Confidential Information to third parties; and Page 2 of 7 (d) not to use any Confidential Information for any purpose except for the Business Purpose without the prior, written consent of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 9 grants the Receiving Party an 'unrestricted, perpetual, and royalty-free license' to use the Confidential Information 'for any purpose whatsoever,' directly contradicting Section 3(d). Section 3(d) states that the Receiving Party may 'not to use any Confidential Information for any purpose except for the Business Purpose without the prior, written consent of the Disclosing Party.' This represents a complete reversal of usage rights, creating significant ambiguity and directly conflicting with the purpose stated in the document. This is a strong contradiction because the changed text explicitly allows any use, while the original text strictly limits use to the Business Purpose with prior consent.
